<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: stable-capability-fields
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/stable-capability-fields.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/stable-capability-fields.qmd@c798c5da7
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Stable Capability Fields

An intelligent system can improve only if it knows what must not change. A new
planner may be faster. A new verifier may be sharper. A new tool-using worker
may solve cases the old one missed. None of that is automatically progress if
the replacement also changes the meaning of the capability, widens the
authority behind it, forgets the evidence that qualified it, or loses the path
back to a known-good state.

Stable Capability Fields are the stack's answer to that problem. They separate
the durable identity of a capability from the replaceable machinery currently
serving it. The field says what capability is being requested, what authority
ceiling it carries, what interface it exposes, what evidence qualifies an
implementation, and what rollback obligations survive replacement. The
implementation may change. The trust contract does not move quietly with it.

This is the bridge from governance into self-improvement. Governance rights ask
what survives institutional change. Stable Capability Fields ask what survives
technical change. In both cases, the point is continuity under pressure:
progress should improve the machinery without moving the terms of trust.

## Problem

A capability name is not a stable boundary by itself. A system can say "planner,"
"retriever," "verifier," "runtime adapter," or "policy updater" while changing
what that component is allowed to see, decide, call, remember, or overwrite. If
every implementation change also changes the capability's meaning, then the
larger system cannot tell whether it has improved or merely redefined success.

The ASI Stack needs a way to let implementations compete and evolve without
letting the capability boundary drift. A field provides that way. It is the
durable semantic boundary that consumers route to and governance reviews.
Implementations are candidates that may satisfy the field under scoped evidence.

The distinction matters because recursive improvement creates constant
replacement pressure. Better models, narrower tools, cheaper routes, stronger
verifiers, and learned policies will all ask to become default. A stable field
turns that pressure into a qualification question: does this candidate satisfy
the same capability, under the same or explicitly changed authority, with
evidence and rollback still attached?

## Why Ordinary Replacement Is Not Enough

Ordinary software and AI practice offers partial answers. A plugin can be
swapped. A model can be upgraded. A service can change versions. A build can
carry provenance. Those disciplines are useful, but none of them alone says
what a capability means inside a governed AI stack.

Semantic Versioning is a useful comparator because it treats a declared public
API as a compatibility boundary and gives breaking changes a visible signal.
But a compatible-looking API does not prove that the new implementation has the
same authority ceiling, evaluator independence, regression memory, route scope,
or rollback readiness.

Capability-based computer systems are a useful comparator because they treat
authority-bearing references as protection-relevant objects. But an
object-capability discipline does not by itself define semantic capability
identity, qualification evidence, lifecycle state, or field-level regression
history for AI components.

SLSA is a useful comparator because artifact identity and provenance matter
before promotion. But supply-chain provenance is not the same as capability
adequacy. A well-attested artifact can still change the field's behavior, widen
authority, satisfy the wrong evaluator, or leave no usable rollback path.

SCF borrows the seriousness of these outside disciplines without claiming to
have implemented them here. In this repository, those sources are comparators:
they help position authority, versioning, and provenance. They do not prove
capability enforcement, API compatibility checking, SLSA conformance, route
validation, evaluator integrity, or rollback execution.

## Core Claim

A stable capability field is a governed boundary with identity, interface,
authority ceiling, qualification evidence, and rollback policy. In the live
book, this claim is a design rationale supported at `argument`, not a deployed
system result.

The source support is architectural. The SCF source supplies the field identity,
versioned contract, content-bound artifact, qualification, route-validation,
lifecycle, authority-ceiling, evaluator-policy, state-migration, and recovery
vocabulary. VIEA supplies the surrounding artifact, support-state, residual,
regression, routing, and permission-envelope context. Talos supplies typed job,
evidence, proof-bundle, replay, and residual-uncertainty context. Ladon and the
Manhattan Protocol supply the authority-handle pressure: sensitive capabilities
need constrained handles, permission checks, compartments, and controlled
injection points. MoECOT remains implementation-reference context only.

That support does not establish production safety, deployed route validation,
deployed authority enforcement, evaluator-integrity enforcement, replacement
safety, rollback execution, benchmark performance, or source-reported runtime
behavior.

## Mechanism

The field record separates three questions that are too often collapsed:

- What capability is being requested?
- Which implementation artifact is being proposed?
- Under what evidence, lifecycle state, and authority may it be routed to?

That separation keeps discovery from becoming self-authorization. A broad
route proposer may nominate a better candidate. A narrow validator decides
whether the candidate still belongs to the field.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  Field["Field contract: identity, interface, authority ceiling"] --> Candidate["Candidate implementation artifact"]
  Field --> Policy["Evaluator policy and route rules"]
  Candidate --> Evidence["Scoped qualification evidence"]
  Evidence --> Lease["Qualification lease"]
  Policy --> Validator["Narrow route validator"]
  Lease --> Validator
  Proposer["Route proposer"] --> Validator
  Validator --> Decision{"Same field and valid route?"}
  Decision -- "yes" --> Lifecycle["Lifecycle: shadow, canary, qualified, default"]
  Decision -- "no" --> Residual["Reject, quarantine, or residual"]
  Lifecycle --> Ledger["Evidence, incidents, regressions"]
  Residual --> Ledger
  Ledger --> Rollback["Rollback and recovery obligations"]
```

The field is the memory of the capability. It carries the semantic boundary,
interface contract, authority ceiling, evaluator policy, evidence references,
regression floors, lifecycle history, incidents, and rollback obligations.
Candidate implementations are replaceable only because the field is not.

Qualification is scoped. A candidate may be qualified for one domain, risk
tier, hardware profile, benchmark epoch, source corpus, authority tier, or user
population without being qualified everywhere. The field should say where that
qualification applies and where it does not.

Qualification also ages. A candidate that passed under one benchmark epoch or
threat model should not stay default forever by inertia. The qualification
lease records whether the claim is active, stale, revoked, or fixture-only; what
review triggers can expire it; which incidents force requalification; and what
rollback obligations remain if the candidate fails.

The route validator is intentionally narrower than the proposer. It checks
field identity, leases, profiles, grants, state paths, composition certificates,
route scope, permission effects, evaluator independence, and authority ceilings
before a candidate becomes default. That is the control point that prevents a
more capable implementation from quietly inheriting trust earned by a weaker
one.

## Interfaces

Different layers see the same field through different faces.

Planning sees a semantic capability boundary. It asks for a field, not for a
random tool or model name.

Execution sees authorized routes. It may invoke only the candidate and route
that are valid for the field's current lifecycle state and authority envelope.

Evidence sees qualification claims, regressions, incidents, readiness gates,
and residuals. It records what was known when a candidate was promoted or
rejected.

Governance sees authority ceilings, evaluator policy, lifecycle transitions,
appeals, incidents, review triggers, and recovery paths. It decides whether a
field can broaden authority, change evaluator policy, or create a new version.

A minimum field record needs stable identity and version fields, an owner,
semantic boundary, interface contract, authority ceiling, implementation
records, lifecycle state, qualification context and status, qualification lease
and lease status, evaluator policy, evaluator independence, route validity,
route scope, route permission effect, consumer policy, evidence references,
readiness gate references, field-history references, incident references,
review triggers, state-migration obligations, regression suite, rollback plan,
rollback obligations, default-route blockers, source references, support-state
effect, and non-claims.

Those fields are not paperwork. They are the handles other stack layers need.
Procedural memory can generate a new tool against a stable target. Benchmark
ratchets can attach evidence to a named capability rather than to a vague sense
of progress. Readiness gates can ask whether the qualification lease is fresh.
Replacement transactions can compare authority deltas instead of assuming a
new implementation inherits the old one's permission.

## Invariants

The first invariant is identity binding: replacing an implementation must not
change the field's semantic boundary unless governance explicitly creates a new
field or version.

The second invariant is authority non-expansion: a replacement cannot gain
broader authority merely because it is more capable. Authority belongs to the
field and its governance record, not to the raw power of the candidate.

The third invariant is evidence locality: qualification evidence belongs to a
field, an implementation artifact, and a context together. Moving any one of
those pieces should downgrade the route until requalification happens.

The fourth invariant is rollback preservation: failed mutation should leave a
known recovery path, residual record, and owner for cleanup.

The fifth invariant is lease aging: qualification should expire, downgrade, or
force review when its recorded triggers fire.

The current Lean hooks model finite record-level versions of two of these
ideas: an implementation may replace another only if it satisfies the field
qualification predicate, and a replacement that expands authority without a
governance grant is rejected. These are useful proof hooks, but they remain
small formal envelopes over declared records. They are not deployed safety
proofs.

## Failure Modes

Field identity drift is the central failure. The name stays stable while the
behavior changes. A consuming layer still calls the same capability, but the
capability now means something else.

Evaluator capture is the qualification failure. A candidate influences the
policy, test, metric, or review process that decides whether it is qualified.
The system then appears to improve because the judge moved.

Authority expansion is the governance failure. A stronger implementation
inherits a route that was safe only for a narrower one.

Orphaned regression memory is the quieter failure. The field accumulated
incidents, tests, edge cases, and recovery knowledge over time, but the suite
was stored with the old implementation. The new candidate passes a fresh demo
while forgetting the history that made the old route trustworthy.

Stale qualification is the time failure. Evidence that was honest in one epoch
continues to authorize default use after the source corpus, threat model,
hardware profile, benchmark regime, user population, or authority tier has
changed.

Provenance laundering is the artifact failure. A candidate carries a clean
build or source trail and is therefore treated as qualified, even though
provenance does not prove semantic identity, evaluator independence, route
safety, or rollback readiness.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The minimum viable SCF is not an autonomous replacement market. It is a public
record schema plus fixtures that make field identity and qualification visible:
one field, two candidate implementations, a preserved regression floor, a
failed canary, an expired qualification lease, an incident-triggered review,
and a rollback path.

The existing `stable_capability_field` schema and synthetic harnesses point in
that direction. They validate record discipline around qualification
predicates, evidence references, readiness references, authority ceilings,
route permission effects, evaluator independence, rollback obligations,
default-route blockers, and non-claim boundaries. The readiness/residual gate
harness also checks synthetic route-validity and rollback-readiness records.

That is useful, but narrow. Schema validation proves record shape. Synthetic
fixtures prove that local validators reject or accept the declared toy records.
They do not prove that a deployed route validator works, that evaluator
integrity is enforced in a live system, that authority is actually contained,
that rollback would execute under pressure, or that source-reported MoECOT
runtime behavior has been reproduced.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature version is a capability-identity and replacement market. In that
system, implementations can improve, split, merge, retire, migrate, or roll
back while the consuming stack keeps knowing which capability is qualified,
under what authority, with which evidence, residuals, and recovery obligations.

In the mature endpoint, a replacement candidate would not become default
because it looks better in a demo. It would carry artifact identity, provenance,
field-version compatibility, scoped qualification evidence, evaluator
independence, authority-delta analysis, regression history, lifecycle state,
incident response, readiness gates, and rollback ownership. The field would
preserve the trust contract while the implementation changes.

That endpoint is still an architecture target. The current book records the
shape of the target and a small set of schemas, fixtures, and Lean hooks. It
does not claim a deployed replacement market, object-capability security
implementation, SemVer checker, SLSA workflow, route validator, evaluator
integrity system, rollback runtime, or MoECOT benchmark reproduction.

## Summary

Stable Capability Fields are upgrade sockets for governed intelligence. They
let a system improve the machinery behind a capability without losing the
capability's identity, authority ceiling, evidence history, evaluator policy,
regression memory, lifecycle state, or rollback obligations.

The chapter's practical lesson is simple: do not let replacement travel by name
alone. A capability should be replaced only when the field can still say what
remained true, what changed, who authorized the change, what evidence qualified
the candidate, what authority it received, what regressions it inherited, and
how to recover if it fails.

## Handoff

Stable fields define what must remain true across implementation changes.
Capability Replacement and Rollback defines how a candidate earns the right to
replace the current implementation. Durable identity becomes a transaction:
qualification evidence, regression floors, residual escrow, monitor windows,
rollback ownership, and authority deltas decide whether improvement can become
a governed state change.
